item_name: Animation Principles for App UX (12 Principles + Brand Motion)
definition_origin: The 12 Principles of Animation were codified by Disney animators Ollie Johnston and Frank Thomas in 'The Illusion of Life' (1981). Val Head's 2016 Smashing/IxDF talks ('What Does Disney Know About Interface Animation Anyway?') translated them to UI. Pasquale D'Silva's 2013 essay 'Transitional Interfaces' established interface-motion as a craft discipline. Rauno Freiberg's 2022-2024 essays codified the modern web/native motion vocabulary (springs, transforms, GPU paths). The brand-motion-personality concept formalizes 'how does this brand move?' as a design-token tier — snappy/soft-spring/bold-cinematic/subtle-craft.
core_purpose: Animation isn't decoration; it's the connective tissue between states that makes UI feel coherent rather than teleporting. Visceral: spring physics with overshoot register as 'alive' in 200ms — pre-conscious recognition. Behavioral: anticipation prepares the user that an action is happening (button press scales down 96% before navigating); follow-through confirms it landed (overshoot + settle). Reflective: the cumulative motion personality across hundreds of micro-interactions becomes brand muscle-memory — users recognize Linear's springs vs Apple's eases vs Discord's bounces blindfolded.
when_needed: Universal — every app has motion, the only choice is whether it's intentional. Critical when: state changes (route transitions, modal open/close), feedback (button press, success/error), continuity (shared element transitions, hero animations), affordance (drag-handle hint, swipe-back). Skip motion when: prefers-reduced-motion is set, on-screen content is dense (don't animate every list-row append), or motion would slow a task (file-upload progress should be linear, not bouncy).
format_options: CSS transitions/animations: declarative, GPU-accelerated for transform/opacity, no JS dependency. CSS @keyframes for reusable named animations. Framer Motion (web React): springs + variants + layout animations + scroll-driven, default-good for app product surfaces. React Native Reanimated 3+: native-thread animations on UI thread, springs + worklets, the only sane choice for RN. React Spring: pure spring physics, tiny bundle. CSS view-transitions API (modern browsers): native shared-element transitions. Lottie/Rive: for complex character/illustration motion (Items 16, 19). GSAP: imperative timeline, best for marketing-page choreography. Motion One (motion.dev): Framer Motion's vanilla-JS sibling, smaller. SwiftUI .animation modifier (iOS native). Jetpack Compose AnimatedVisibility (Android native). Hierarchy: prefer transform/opacity (compositor-only); avoid animating width/height/top/left (layout-thrash).
file_size_performance: Framer Motion: ~30-40 KB gzipped — fine for product surfaces, heavy for landing pages. Motion One: ~5 KB — ideal for marketing. CSS-only: 0 KB JS. React Spring: ~15 KB. Reanimated 3: bundled with RN, no extra. Performance budget: keep simultaneous transform animations <8 on a screen; avoid animating box-shadow (paint, expensive); avoid animating filter (compositor-bypass on some browsers). Target 60fps minimum, 120fps where supported (iOS ProMotion, Android high-refresh). Use will-change sparingly (memory cost). For Reanimated, run on UI thread via worklets.
free_tools: Framer Motion (free, open-source, MIT): industry-standard for React, springs + variants + layout transitions + AnimatePresence + scroll. React Spring (free, MIT): pure-spring API. Motion One / motion.dev (free, MIT): vanilla JS + React + Vue, smaller than Framer Motion. GSAP (free for non-commercial; club $99/yr for commercial premium plugins) — most plugins now free under GreenSock's 2024 license-update. AutoAnimate (free): zero-config auto-animation for list reorders. Lenis (free): smooth-scroll. Theatre.js (free, open-source): visual animation editor with code export. CSS view-transitions API (free, browser-native). Reanimated 3 (free, RN-included). React Native Skia (free): canvas-based animations, springs, parametric. Easings.net (free reference). Keyframes.app (free CSS keyframe builder). Cubic-bezier.com (free easing curve preview). Motion examples library (https://motion.dev/examples free). Anime.js (free, MIT): timeline-based JS lib.
paid_tools: GSAP Club ($99-199/yr): premium plugins (SplitText, MorphSVG, DrawSVG) — only worth it for marketing-heavy or scroll-driven landing pages. Theatre.js Pro (donation-based, free for most). Framer (the design tool, $5-25/seat/mo): visual prototyping with code-export to Framer Motion. Rive Pro ($16-32/seat/mo): when motion crosses into character animation (see Item 19). Tiering: starter $0/mo (Framer Motion + CSS + Motion One — sufficient for 95% of apps). Pro $99/yr (GSAP Club) — for committed marketing-motion practice. Enterprise $50+/mo (Framer Pro + Rive Pro + GSAP Business) — only for design-engineering teams.
gemini_pro_workflow: Step 1 — Brand-motion personality definition. Prompt Gemini Pro: 'My app archetype is {archetype}. Define a brand-motion-personality token system with: 4 named easing curves (snappy/soft-spring/bold-cinematic/subtle-craft) as cubic-bezier values, spring physics presets (stiffness/damping/mass), default durations per scale (micro 100-200ms, macro 300-500ms, expressive 600-900ms). Output as design tokens.' Step 2 — Per-component motion spec. Prompt: 'For a {component_e_g_modal} in a {archetype} app, write the open/close animation spec: scale, opacity, translateY, easing, duration, child-stagger-delay. Apply Disney 12-principles relevant: anticipation, follow-through, slow-in/slow-out.' Step 3 — Veo for motion-reference videos: 'Modal entering screen with bouncy spring overshoot from below, 400ms, on a clean app background, side-by-side with same modal entering linearly for comparison.' Step 4 — Critique pass via Gemini multimodal: paste a screen-recording of your animation + ask 'Identify motion problems by 12-principles. Score anticipation, slow-in/slow-out, follow-through, arcs, secondary motion. List exact ms-level fixes.' Step 5 — Generate code: 'Write Framer Motion variants implementing the {archetype} brand-motion tokens for: button press, modal open, list-item enter, route transition.' Custom Gem: 'Motion Director' Gem with system prompt 'You are Val Head. For each animation request, output Disney-12-principles application + spring physics + ms timing + Framer Motion / Reanimated code.'
workflow_steps: 1. Define brand archetype (carry from brand work, ~0 min if already done). 2. Pick motion personality (15 min): snappy / soft-spring / bold-cinematic / subtle-craft — write the 1-line description. 3. Define easing token set (30 min): 4-6 named cubic-beziers + 3-4 spring presets (stiffness, damping, mass) + duration scale (100/200/300/400/600 ms). 4. Map components to motion (45 min): button-press / hover / focus / modal / sheet / route-transition / toast / list-item — assign personality + duration to each. 5. Implement core primitives (90-180 min): a <Press /> wrapper with default press-scale, a <Modal /> with brand-motion entrance, a <Sheet /> with spring-pull, route-transition wrapper. 6. Apply Disney 12-principles per component: anticipation (button scales down 96% before navigating), follow-through (modal overshoots then settles), slow-in/slow-out (default cubic-bezier on every transform), exaggeration (celebration animations — bigger than realistic). 7. Reduced-motion fallbacks (30 min): every motion respects prefers-reduced-motion → either no motion or shorter cross-fade. 8. Test on 60fps + 120fps devices + low-end Android. 9. Document in Storybook with motion-spec page. 10. Lock motion tokens in design system (figma-tokens / Tailwind theme).
ai_prompt_templates: Brand-motion-token prompt: 'Generate brand-motion-personality tokens for a {archetype} app: easing curves named snappy/soft-spring/bold-cinematic/subtle-craft as cubic-bezier values OR spring(stiffness, damping, mass) values, durations micro/macro/expressive in ms, list 5 example components with applied tokens. Format as JSON design tokens.' Component-motion prompt: 'For component {component} in {archetype} app, write the entrance and exit animation spec: starting state (opacity, transform), ending state, easing, duration, stagger if multi-child. Apply Disney 12-principles: anticipation, follow-through, slow-in/slow-out where appropriate. Output as Framer Motion variants object AND React Native Reanimated equivalent.' Critique prompt: 'Critique this animation video by Disney 12-principles. Score 0-10 each: anticipation (wind-up before action), slow-in/slow-out (acceleration curves), follow-through (overshoot + settle), arcs (curved motion paths not linear), secondary motion (offset of dependent parts), timing (ms duration appropriateness), exaggeration (more than realistic where needed), appeal (memorability). List 3 ms-level fixes.' Spring-physics prompt: 'Recommend spring(stiffness, damping, mass, initial-velocity) for a {motion_type} on a {archetype} mascot/UI. Output 3 variants — snappy, bouncy, settled — with overshoot percentage and settle ms.'
claude_code_integration: Install: pnpm add framer-motion (web) OR react-native-reanimated react-native-gesture-handler (RN). Token storage: /packages/design-tokens/motion.ts as the single source of truth. Claude Code can: (1) generate the motion-token TypeScript file from a JSON spec (easings, springs, durations); (2) emit a <Press />, <Modal />, <Sheet />, <RouteTransition /> primitive set wired to tokens; (3) write a usePrefersReducedMotion() hook + automatic fallback; (4) generate Storybook stories for every motion variant; (5) write an ESLint rule that flags raw cubic-bezier() in component code (force token use); (6) generate a motion-debug overlay (visualizes timing in dev mode); (7) auto-port web Framer Motion variants to Reanimated 3 worklets; (8) write a perf benchmark script that profiles fps during route transitions on Pixel-3 emulator.
emotional_design_integration: Snappy (Sage/Hero — Linear, Vercel, Stripe): cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1), durations 150-250ms, decisive, no overshoot. 'Get out of the way' personality. Soft-spring (Caregiver — Headspace, Calm, Finch): spring(stiffness:120, damping:18, mass:1), 300-500ms, gentle settle, breathing-like. 'I am here for you' personality. Bold-cinematic (Magician/Hero — Apple, Arc, Pi.ai): spring(stiffness:200, damping:22, mass:1.2) + dramatic durations 400-800ms, large transforms, hero scale changes. 'This is an event' personality. Subtle-craft (Sage — Notion, Things 3): 100-200ms, near-imperceptible, ease-out cubic. 'Trust the craft' personality. Jester (Duolingo, Discord): cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) friendly-overshoot, exaggerated rubber-band, 250-400ms, secondary squash. The same component (modal) animates radically differently per archetype — and that difference is the brand.
specific_examples: Linear (https://linear.app) — masterclass in subtle-craft snappy motion: every keyboard-shortcut, every state-change, ~150ms cubic-bezier, no excess. Stripe Checkout — premium snappy ease-outs, 200ms transitions, never bouncy. Apple iOS (system-wide) — bold-cinematic springs everywhere, the reference standard for spring(0.5, 825, 30, 0) UIView springs. Discord (https://discord.com) — Jester rubber-band on emoji reactions. Headspace — soft-spring on every screen, breathing rhythm encoded in motion. Things 3 (Cultured Code, https://culturedcode.com/things) — subtle-craft microinteractions, the gold standard for restrained app motion. Arc Browser — bold-cinematic playful springs. Duolingo — Jester mascot springs + UI springs. Nuage's design playground (Vercel showcase). Rauno Freiberg's site (https://rauno.me) — pedagogical motion examples. Linear's blog post on motion (https://linear.app/blog). Pasquale D'Silva's 'Transitional Interfaces' (https://medium.com/@pasql/transitional-interfaces-926eb80d64e3) — foundational essay.
anti_ai_slop_traps: (1) Default linear easing on transforms — instantly cheap, no exception. Counter: ALWAYS use ease-out at minimum for things entering, ease-in for exiting. (2) Same easing curve everywhere — flattens brand. Counter: 4-6 named tokens applied per component-class. (3) Excessive overshoot on every interaction — feels like a playground. Counter: reserve overshoot for moments that deserve celebration; default to no-overshoot ease. (4) Animating to and from the wrong properties (width/height/top/left causes layout-thrash). Counter: animate only transform + opacity + filter. (5) Forgetting prefers-reduced-motion — accessibility violation + AppStore reviewer-risk. (6) Stagger on lists with no rhythm — staggers feel random; use a consistent 30-50ms cascade. (7) Spring physics with damping too low — bounces forever, looks unresolved; counter: damping 18-30 for natural settle. (8) Duration > 500ms for utility actions — feels slow. Reserve >500ms for celebrations only. (9) Motion-on-everything fatigue — counter: be ruthless about which interactions deserve motion. (10) AI-generated motion specs that prescribe linear or default values — counter-prompt: 'never use linear easing, never duration <100ms or >800ms for utility, always specify overshoot or no-overshoot intentionally.'
common_pitfalls: Performance: animating box-shadow / filter / borderRadius on iOS Safari causes paint thrash — prefer compositor-only properties (transform, opacity). Layout-thrash: animating top/left/width/height triggers layout — use translate/scale. Reanimated worklet trap: closures can't capture non-worklet values without 'worklet' directive. RN-Web mismatches: same Framer Motion code may not work on react-native-web — use Reanimated for cross-platform RN+Web. Spring overshoot infinity: damping too low causes endless oscillation — clamp via Reanimated's restDisplacementThreshold. Reduced-motion ignored: most motion libs don't honor it by default — wire usePrefersReducedMotion() + override duration to 0. ProMotion 120fps gotcha: keyframe animations encoded at 30fps look choppy on iPhone Pro — let spring physics interpolate smoothly. Memory: stale animation listeners. Scope: animating every component is fatigue, not delight.
accessibility_notes: Honor prefers-reduced-motion via useReducedMotion() (Framer Motion) or window.matchMedia (CSS) — fall back to opacity-only or instant. Avoid >3Hz flashing. Provide a global motion-toggle in app settings (independent of OS preference). Don't rely on motion alone to communicate state changes — pair with text/icon. Vestibular: avoid parallax + large transforms when reduced-motion is set. Focus management: animated focus rings should be detectable by keyboard users.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Web: Framer Motion / Motion One / GSAP / CSS — all run on main thread (Framer Motion uses compositor for transform/opacity). RN: Reanimated 3 runs on UI thread via worklets — fundamentally different mental model, but produces 60fps+ animations independent of JS thread blocking. iOS native: SwiftUI animations + UIKit springs use UIKit's spring(0.5, 825, 30, 0) constants. Android native: Compose AnimatedVisibility + spring(). 120fps: iOS ProMotion auto-scales spring physics; Android high-refresh varies. Touch: mobile gestures (pan/pinch/swipe) need react-native-gesture-handler or web's pointer events with passive listeners. View transitions API: modern web only — no RN equivalent yet.
sources: [Val Head — What Does Disney Know About Interface Animation? (2016)](https://valhead.com/2016/01/18/what-does-disney-know-about-interface-animation-anyway/); [IxDF — Disney's 12 Principles to UI Design](https://ixdf.org/literature/article/ui-animation-how-to-apply-disney-s-12-principles-of-animation-to-ui-design); [Disney's 12 Principles in Everyday UI (Bootcamp Medium)](https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/disneys-12-principles-of-animation-in-everyday-ui-design-71c6592064fe); [The Physics Behind Spring Animations (Maxime Heckel)](https://blog.maximeheckel.com/posts/the-physics-behind-spring-animations/); [Effortless UI Spring Animations: A Two-Parameter Approach (Kvin Me)](https://www.kvin.me/posts/effortless-ui-spring-animations); [Designing spring animations for the web (Felix Runquist)](https://felixrunquist.com/posts/designing-spring-animations-for-the-web); [Demystifying UIKit Spring Animations (Christian Schnorr, Medium)](https://medium.com/ios-os-x-development/demystifying-uikit-spring-animations-2bb868446773); [Motion.dev spring API](https://motion.dev/docs/spring); [Apple SwiftUI interpolatingSpring docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/animation/interpolatingspring(mass:stiffness:damping:initialvelocity:)); [Pasquale D'Silva — Transitional Interfaces](https://medium.com/@pasql/transitional-interfaces-926eb80d64e3); [Rauno Freiberg's interface motion writing](https://rauno.me)
